result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story begins—before the first sentence, before the alphabet arranges itself into meaning. There is a hush, a kind of cosmic pause, like the breath a violinist takes before the bow touches the string. In that hush, every possible story exists at once: every heartbreak, every galaxy, every mundane Tuesday that will later be remembered as the day everything changed. Then the page intervenes, murdering almost all of them, leaving only the thin trail of words we call “what happened.” It feels cruel, but maybe it’s also mercy; no one could survive the full thunder of unfiltered possibility.

I like to picture an alternate physics where forgotten possibilities don’t vanish but drift outward like dandelion seeds, rooting in the cracks of other minds. The joke you didn’t tell at the party lands in a stranger’s shower thought three cities away. The road you didn’t take becomes the setting of a child’s dream. Over decades, whole weather systems of unchosen paths churn above us, raining deja-vu and inexplicable longing onto picnics and funerals and 2 a.m. convenience-store parking lots.

Yesterday I found a 1976 polaroid wedged behind a drawer at the thrift store: a boy in a crocheted sweater holding up a plastic dinosaur, both of them glaring at the camera as if they already distrust nostalgia. Someone wrote “Casey—first day of the world” on the back. I carried it around the aisles like evidence, half expecting the fluorescent lights to buzz louder until a hidden narrator revealed why this fragment matters. Instead I bought it for 49 cents, took it home, and propped it against the windowsill, where sunset lit the dinosaur’s tiny amber eye. Now it feels like the apartment has a new roommate who never speaks but remembers birthdays I’ve forgotten.

I wonder if memory itself is just an obsolete filing system, if one day we’ll evolve past it the way we lost gills. Maybe future children will be born with a tidy protocol: experience, extract lesson, discard event. No clutter, no scar tissue, no phantom limbs of April afternoons that smelled like cut grass and someone’s mother’s lemon cleanser. They’ll move through the world sleek as dolphins, while we old-model humans keep hoarding ticket stubs and opening drawers to stare at dead phones that still hold the last text from people who became geography.

Sometimes I write because I’m trying to manufacture a wormhole back to that pre-sentence hush—to stand in the lobby of everything that could still be true. I never manage it; the moment I press the first key, the turnstile clicks and yet another future pins me against the wall. But even failure leaves a residue, a faint aurora on the inside of my skull. I walk around lit by that private glow, a dim walking yes, and for a few hours the city feels like a manuscript I haven’t ruined yet.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
